item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image within this article is non-compliant as the original source is here. As such, the image has been removed.
Congrats for reaching 200! This skip really deserves it, so well written and interesting. Upvoted

item.title: Re: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded: BROOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOO THIS IS AMAZING

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is a prime example of "Innocuous until you get to the twist." I absolutely love how it never says anything about the subjects being alive or dead to lull you into complacency and then pulls the rug out from under you in the final tests. I also don't mind a little incompetence from my Foundation once in a blue moon. Good comedy often comes from failure, and we don't get too much of that from a 100% Hyper-Competent Foundation.

I hope you enjoy the reading!

https://youtu.be/e4_n6BMCLoU

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are the historical head aware about their lives? I.e do they remember what they've done during life and their death s? Good SCP +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That guillotine end twist was unexpected. One of the best short entries in this contest that manages to pull the scary angle off quite well. Sometimes less really is more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a perfect SCP, perfect item, perfect twist, perfect contest entry.

Really great, and one of my favorite entries in this big pool of shorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I somehow didn't get the joke until I read the comments, at which point I promptly freaked the fsck out and called my own mother to confirm she was all right, jfc, this skip is going to give me nightmares.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm. This is fun. +1

That said - I would have liked to seen at least one more test. IE. Have an intact corpse of a person in observation, and asked the subject to think of the person the corpse belonged to.

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded: *finger guns* I like it.

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded: How about Head Cabinet?

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded: Ah, yeah that makes sense. I use the Wikidot Search or Google (or author pages), and I am only dimly aware of the chatbot functionality.

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded: There technically isn't, but it can cause problems mainly in IRC when you search by title using Alexandra. There's been precedence before.

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded:
I don't think there's a rule about not sharing titles.

But if you want a 2nd order pun:"Popsicle" or "Radio Head".

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded:
If you change this:

Restful Meadows Funeral Home and Crematory, went out of business c. 1976

To this:

Restful Meadows Funeral Home and Crematory, went out of business c. 1977

You could name your skip Talking Heads: 77.

item.title: Re: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded:
Damn! Half the reason I wrote it was for the pun!

Ah well. Anyone have any suggestions?

item.title: Uh, you may want to rename this one
item.content:encoded: Second time I've seen this happen. First it was with the hoop snakes, which were named in the mainlist as "Ouroboros"… which already existed. Same goes for this one, which shares its title with SCP-1263. It's a wonderful title, but Goodwill got dibs on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At least Dr. Wu can see her mom again by using this SCP.

…or…

…oh god, the Foundation isn't going to let her use this thing again.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yup. I like this. I like it a lot. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Prior to that, the assumption is that it's summoning dead heads due to being set in a morgue.

Only if this bit hadn't been read:

while thinking of another either living or dead sentient creature.

That specifically states the head can be a living or dead head, before summoning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article itself really doesn't confirm/deny anything. Wu just thought she perhaps killed her mother.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I will no vote, because:
1. Personally I think it is hard to believe that Dr. Wu would not call her mother instead of using a newly discovered anomaly without permission.
2. It is implied all living beings whose heads are summoned die as a result of blood loss, but the D-class is the only subject whose head does not disappear, so it would be reasonable to assume only the D-class died as a result of being in the same room as the SCP.
However, I may have misread the last test, if the D-class head did disappear, that is not clear from the text.
3. On the other hand, I like the idea that the Foundation has imbedded agents on ghost hunting TV shows, and the implication that sometimes that actually leads to containing an anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not at all. But it isn't until the reveal with the uncle that we're told, explicitly, that the subjects are alive prior to being summoned into the cabinet. Prior to that, the assumption is that it's summoning dead heads due to being set in a morgue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I am being dense for thinking that decapitation is a reason to think that they didn't remain alive and well after the test…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everyone seems to think that all the heads seen in tests n° 1 to 3 belonged to dead creatures.

It's an incorrect implication due to the setting of the scip being a morgue. It's a rather masterful play on our perceptions and expectations by the author. Scip is set in a morgue, so, logically, the anomaly affects dead people. Just another example of why this object is so good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The terrier, the uncle and Dr Wu's mom were all alive and well from what we know, and there is no reason they didn't remain so after the test!

The last test. The subject's body was present and was exsanguinating while the head was missing, and functional, and the head was not returned to the body when the test completed. It is reasonable to suspect that this occurs in every test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The picture is from the Visual Records Wiki. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait wait wait, there's something I don't get here.

Everyone seems to think that all the heads seen in tests n° 1 to 3 belonged to dead creatures. But where does the article mention this? See spoilers from Ahbonjour, above! The terrier, the uncle and Dr Wu's mom were all alive and well from what we know, and there is no reason they didn't remain so after the test!
… um. Unless Dr Wu lifter her mom's head for a goodbye kiss, of course… <shudder>

The collie was dead from the start… and the head starts bleeding when it's lifted from the shelf…
The D-class in test 5…

Mmh. This is rather horrible, when you think about it.
Even when you don't, in fact.

Take your +1, ahbonjour. You've earned it!!!!

(where did you find the picture? It's rather… um… I'll get back to you with the right word…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, the apparent incompetency is something that I had to use because of word constraints. When this contest is over I'd like to go back and fix it up.

spoilers
– hide block

The reason Dr. Wu opens up the cabinet in the first place is one of the inspirations for this skip. I have a friend who has technologically challenged parents in India and therefore, the only means of contact they have is over the telephone. Long distance calls, at least that long distance, is hella expensive! My friend now has a phone plan that supports this, but it too is pretty damn expensive.

So, when Dr. Wu was presented with a way to speak to
her mom overseas, for free, with no apparent ill effects to her mom, she jumped on it. I mean, who knows how long it's been since they've spoken. Yes, she probably could have waited on more tests, but love is rarely logical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done. Nice mixture of fucking creepy and extremely weird, which hits the notes of uncanny horror that I will always enjoy in works on this site. It captures a nice sense of essential wrong-ness that marks some of the better articles on the site. The stinger at the end, despite my issues, is a nice narrative flourish. Overall, a solid piece that I think was enabled by the contest parameters.

That said, I have a bit of a hard time with the twist at the end. It displays an amount of incredible stupidity on the part of the researcher involved, and while I know "incompetent Foundation" is a popular trope amongst some, it's never made any sense to me for quite a few reasons. Any number of things must have been running through the minds of the researchers working on this. I cannot imagine "hey, I should invoke my own mother with this crazy teleporting head thing that doesn't obey the laws of god and man" would ever be one of them.

Despite that, the storytelling skill involved overcomes my objections. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this. Dr. Wu's experiment made me wonder what were the ramifications…lifting the dog's head "killed" it…hmm…Then the OH SHIT. I'm guessing Mom was still alive before the test?? eek.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, once this is over, I'm going to come back and expand a little more. Not too much, don't want to lose the much of the mystery and pacing. Enough to get out there what I really wanted to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Once this contest is over, I'd like this scip to be revisited. Does it work on heads of people already deceased, or did we kill two dogs testing this out (along with a D-Class, Dr. Wu's mother, and possibly D-8452's uncle)?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a plot hole, that's implying something rather than stating it outright. Sometimes less is more, and subtlety is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

ghost-hunting show [REDACTED]

FYI, had you use blackbox instead, you could've reduced the word count by one (wordcounter.net does not regard blackbox as words). It's minor, but I want to address it.

Pros:

  • I actually find the recovery of this SCP funny that I can support it.
  • The test logs are something worthy of interest.

Cons:

  • Usage of blackboxes could be better than [REDACTED], but that's largely preference.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking this was boring, but OH MAMMA THEM TESTS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Closing SCP-2733 does not result in D-8452's head remanifesting on its neck.

Very good, congrats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, okay. I tend to read these as complete published studies, so it seemed like an obvious failure to follow up but that isn't necessarily the case. Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my scan on it, as well. Once we know what it does and where the heads come from, it's pretty obvious a search would be done for the uncle and possibly the dogs. Adding it would be a needless detail, imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, like I mentioned earlier up, this is my take on it. We all know what happened to the uncle, we all know that the Foundation will check into it, and anything added to it takes focus off of the main hook in the story.

I vote for 'Leave as is', ahbonjour :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't put dates because they took up space but in my head, all of the experiments listed took place in the same day. This article is a snapshot in time. I'm certain they looked for the uncle and found him afterwards, but he's not the focus of the story and it's clear to the reader what happened to him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "The Foundation was unable to locate this person" as a footnote only adds eight words. [REDACTED] as a footnote only adds one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't see what follow up or plot hole exists. It's apparent what happened to D-8452's uncle, and obviously the Foundation will do what they need to if anyone investigates his death too closely.

It doesn't need anything, and the death of the D-class's uncle, while bad for the uncle of course, isn't really the interesting hook of the story, in my opinion.

I think it works fine as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The uncle is evidence about what happens to the bodies (so are the dogs but harder to locate). Wouldn't the Foundation be interested in this information. Sudden decapitation should be on their radar anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plot hole? Not seeing it, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably, but I only had 500 words. We'll see if I can find a way to make it work before the posting period is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, but I can't really not see it as minor plot hole in something otherwise excellent even some slight lampshading would be fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. No follow up. Let it remain a mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I was digging it too, ready to give it a bit of thought before voting…

Dr. Wu becomes distressed…

ALL THE +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't there be follow up about the one subject's uncle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There I am, peacefully reading along, thinking this is a good article. Then I get to the part about the unsanctioned test. Huh, thinks I, that seems a little unprofessional of the good Dr. Wu, but who doesn't wish they could have that one last conversation with their deceased parent? Read last test. Oh, sh— right alongside Dr. Wu.

Have your +1 and enjoy it with your wonderfully paced self.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's really creepy is that we can buy caskets at Walmart.

Short works entry, last count at 496 words.

E: Edited following contest end. Sits at 550 now. Added things I wanted to be there in any case.

E: New picture borrowed from Pavel Tcholakov on Flickr.
